blocks.tagline: A seized truss rod, a fifty-year-old neck, and one careful extraction.

blocks.html:
The bass came in dead straight in the worst way — the neck had a forward bow no amount of truss-rod turning would touch, because the rod itself had stopped turning. Somewhere in the last fifty years the nut had seized to the threads, and the previous tech's answer had been to lean on it until something gave. Something did: the rod, not the bow.

Before touching a wrench I wanted to know what I was working with. A '68 Jazz neck is a single truss rod anchored at the heel, adjusted at the nut. If the threads are gone, the rod has to come out the heel end, and that means pulling the fingerboard — or finding another way.

blocks.image_text: The seized rod backed out a half turn, then bound solid. You can see the galling on the exposed threads where it had been forced.

blocks.html: The trick that saved the board: heat, penetrating oil wicked down the channel overnight, and a purpose-ground driver that bit the rod's flats instead of the rounded-off nut. It came free in stages over two days. No drama, just patience — which is most of this trade.

blocks.caption: Full bench walkthrough: the extraction, re-threading the anchor, and the re-set.

blocks.html: With the rod out and the anchor re-cut, the neck took relief adjustment normally for the first time in decades. Strung back up to pitch it held dead-on overnight — the real test. The owner gets his bass back; you get the long version on the bench above.

meta.author_about: Dan Erlewine has repaired and built stringed instruments for more than fifty years and has written some of the trade's most-used reference work on the bench craft of guitar repair.
